segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with commentators setting the stage for the "last heat, timed final of the 200 Medley Relay," discussing "two different times and records" over a backdrop of lively crowd noise and background music. The dynamic and celebratory entrance sequence for the Men's 200 Yard Medley Relay at an NCAA swimming event begins as teams emerge one by one through branded tunnels, accompanied by dramatic smoke effects and team-specific visuals. First, the Florida State team, wearing black swimwear and hoodies, confidently steps out, with commentators announcing they will be in lane one. Next, Indiana’s team, in black hoodies and red-and-white striped pants, pumps their fists and gestures energetically, identified by commentators as "the Indiana swim team, the famed candy stripe," with swimmers Bar, Benzing, Brooks, and King highlighted. Texas follows in white tracksuits, one swimmer raising a blue water bottle, as commentators introduce their "brilliant swimmers" Maudlin, German, Present, Hubert Kosh, and Olympian Chris Giuliano, a transfer from Notre Dame. Tennessee’s team, in black jackets and swim trunks, walks out with a relaxed demeanor, one holding a towel, while commentators state they will be in lane four, featuring Ghi Caribe on the third leg and Jordan Crooks on the fourth. Florida’s team, in black hoodies with Gator logos, makes a bold entrance with hand gestures and head movements, with commentators emphasizing the significant presence of Josh Liendo, noting his absence from their SEC relay but his inclusion here. Finally, Arizona State’s team, dressed in white tracksuits, raises their arms in unison as they step out. The entire scene is lively, with event staff, ESPN camera crews, and seated officials visible along the poolside, underscoring the competitive and theatrical atmosphere of the NCAA championship.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video captures a dynamic and celebratory entrance sequence for the Men's 200 Yard Medley Relay at an NCAA swimming event, broadcasted by ESPN. Teams from Florida State, Indiana, Texas, Tennessee, Florida, and Arizona State make their grand entrances one by one through branded tunnels, each accompanied by dramatic smoke effects and team-specific visuals. The Florida State team, wearing black swimwear and hoodies, emerges first, followed by Indiana’s team in black hoodies and red-and-white striped pants, who pump their fists and gesture confidently. Texas follows in white tracksuits, with one swimmer raising a blue water bottle in the air. Tennessee’s team, in black jackets and swim trunks, walks out with a relaxed demeanor, one holding a towel. Florida’s team, in black hoodies with Gator logos, makes a bold entrance with hand gestures and head movements. Finally, Arizona State’s team, dressed in white tracksuits, raises their arms in unison as they step out. The environment is lively, with event staff, cameras, and seated officials visible along the poolside, emphasizing the competitive and theatrical atmosphere of the NCAA championship.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Arizona State Team, dressed in white tracksuits and hoodies, emerging energetically from a smoke-filled, branded tunnel displaying names like Vergnes, Dobrzanski, and Kulw, as commentators highlight swimmer Ilya Kharun's presence on the third leg. They raise their arms in unison, showcasing unity, before the scene shifts to the California Team, confidently walking past a large 'CALIFORNIA' banner featuring names like Seeliger and Alexy. The commentators note that Cal will be in lane seven with Jack Alexy on the anchor leg, mentioning three returning swimmers from last year's fourth-place finish. Next, the NC State Team enters through their tunnel, wearing white tracksuits with red accents and swim caps, one with a red beanie, displaying focused composure, with commentators confirming their position in lane eight. As the teams move toward the pool deck, swimmers begin preparing for the race, now in swim caps and goggles, adjusting gear and stretching on starting blocks within a large indoor aquatic center adorned with '2025 NCAA SWIMMING & DIVING' banners. Event Officials observe from the poolside, and the ESPN Camera Crew captures the action. Commentators continue to discuss the race's third leg, mentioning Hubert Kosh, Kabe, and Josh Liendo, and referencing past performances and relay details, including Tennessee and Jordan Crooks anchoring, building anticipation for the "SEC matchup" unfolding in the pool.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of shirtless swimmers in swim caps and goggles on starting blocks, adjusting gear and stretching for the 200Y Medley Relay. The camera pulls back to reveal eight lanes marked with team names and logos: Florida St (lane 1), Indiana (lane 2), Texas (lane 3), Tennessee (lane 4), Florida (lane 5), Arizona St (lane 6), California (lane 7), and NC State (lane 8). Two announcers provide enthusiastic commentary, identifying Texas, Tennessee, and Florida as potential top three contenders, while noting Indiana's potential to break the American record despite being fourth, and Arizona State's anticipated strength in the later legs, also referring to California as 'Cal.' The atmosphere is intense under bright arena lighting, with officials observing from the poolside and the ESPN logo visible. An official's voice commands, 'Take your marks,' followed by a distinct starting beep as the swimmers dive into the pool in unison, initiating the race. As the camera follows the swimmers executing the butterfly stroke, NC State takes an early lead. The on-screen timer tracks the race progress, displaying times like 9.89, 13.5, and 20.24 seconds, while the leaderboard updates to show NC State in the lead. The announcers excitedly call out the action, highlighting a 9.89 split from McCarty of North Carolina State, questioning if anyone can break the fastest backstroke split in history at 20.07, and noting the time to beat is 1:22.01, with the crowd audible in the background.

The atmosphere is intense, with the crowd visible in the background and the pool illuminated under bright arena lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The intense final stretch of the 200Y Medley Relay at the 2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships unfolds under bright arena lighting, filled with the roar of the crowd and the energetic, fast-paced commentary of the race announcer. The camera pans across the pool, capturing swimmers executing powerful freestyle strokes. The announcer highlights key moments, mentioning "German Prez of Texas" who was initially second, then correcting to "Will Modlin" with a 20.2 split, and noting "Crooks of Texas" in third. He excitedly exclaims about "Julian Smith" achieving a "perfect start" with a 22.1 split. The "big battle on the fly" sees "Leando of Florida" taking the lead with an impressive "19 flat" split. The on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically update, showing Florida leading at 1:20.05, followed closely by Tennessee at 1:20.50 and Texas at 1:20.28. The announcer identifies "Scott Buff" as the Florida swimmer maintaining the lead, noting that "Crooks in Tennessee" is the only team that can catch them. As the race culminates, the announcer's excitement peaks, declaring "it's Florida! Scott Buff comes in at 1:20!" The Florida team, in black hoodies with Gator logos, emerges victorious, celebrating as one swimmer raises an arm upon reaching the wall, while event officials and team members watch intently from the bustling poolside.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video captures the intense final stretch of the 200Y Medley Relay at the 2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships. Swimmers from multiple teams are seen executing the freestyle leg of the relay, with the camera panning across the pool to follow their progress. The on-screen timer and leaderboard update in real-time, showing Florida taking the lead with a time of 1:20.05, followed closely by Tennessee at 1:20.50 and Texas at 1:20.28. The swimmers display powerful strokes and strong finishes, with some reaching the wall and turning to hand off to their teammates. The poolside is bustling with activity: coaches, officials, and team members watch intently, while the crowd in the stands cheers. The atmosphere is electric, with the final moments of the race unfolding under bright arena lighting. The Florida team emerges victorious, celebrating as the results are displayed on screen.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a brief, unrelated clip from 'World of Warships,' showing a naval battle with a warship firing in a snowy coastal environment, before transitioning to the high-energy 2025 NCAA Men's Swimming & Diving Championships. The focus shifts to the Men's 200Y Medley Relay, where swimmers, including those from Florida in black hoodies with Gator logos and swim caps, Tennessee in black jackets, and Texas in white tracksuits, are seen completing the freestyle leg with powerful strokes and turns. The on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically update, showing Florida as the leader. As the race concludes, the Race Announcer, visible poolside in a white shirt, excitedly declares the final time of "05" (1:20.05), proclaiming it the "fastest in history" and that "all the records smashed," though noting it's "under review." The leaderboard confirms Florida in first, Tennessee in second, and Texas in third. The announcer continues, stating, "Just what we predicted, my friend. 1-2-3 SEC! I said Florida was going to win this, they did," crediting "Liendo" for the win. Following their NCAA Record-breaking victory, the Florida team celebrates enthusiastically, raising arms, hugging, and smiling broadly, with one swimmer lying on the pool deck, exhausted but triumphant. They then don white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd and displaying immense joy. The bright and lively poolside environment is adorned with NCAA banners, spectators, and event officials, with the ESPN logo visible, indicating the broadcast.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a brief, unrelated clip from the game 'World of Warships,' showing a naval battle with a warship firing at enemy targets in a snowy coastal environment, before transitioning to the 2025 NCAA Men's Swimming & Diving Championships. The focus shifts to the Men's 200Y Medley Relay, where swimmers are seen completing the freestyle leg of the relay in a high-energy, competitive atmosphere. The Florida team, wearing black hoodies with Gator logos and swim caps, dominates the race, finishing first with a time of 1:20.05, setting a new NCAA Record. The on-screen leaderboard confirms Florida in first, Tennessee in second, and Texas in third. After the race, the Florida team celebrates enthusiastically, with swimmers raising arms, hugging, and smiling broadly. One swimmer is seen lying on the pool deck, exhausted but triumphant, while teammates gather around him. The team then dons white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd and displaying joy. The poolside is lined with NCAA banners, and the event is broadcast by ESPN, as indicated by the logo in the corner. The environment is bright and lively, with spectators in the stands and event officials observing. The swimmers on starting blocks are now seen in the pool, completing their freestyle strokes with powerful turns at the wall. The Race Announcer, in a white shirt and dark pants, is visible near the poolside, providing commentary. The on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically update throughout the race, showing the final results and the NCAA Record achievement.

segments.integrated_caption: The intense atmosphere of the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships unfolds with a high-angle view of multiple swimmers executing powerful freestyle strokes and turns in a relay, churning the water with splashes as event officials observe from the poolside. The arena's full scale is revealed with 'NCAA' and 'SWIMMING & DIVING CHAMPIONSHIPS' banners, while the stands are packed with cheering spectators. A large digital scoreboard confirms the 'MEN 200Y MEDLEY RELAY' with Florida leading, as the race announcer's voice cuts through the crowd noise, discussing a potential official review regarding a swimmer's feet being 'still on the block' during a critical exchange, noting the difficulty in verifying from certain angles and the reliance on 'electronic necessities.' Teams like Florida State (in black swimwear), Texas (in white tracksuits), and Tennessee (in black jackets) are visible near the starting blocks or in the pool. The announcer then specifically details the Florida team's 'exchange from fly to free,' identifying Liendo coming in and Scott Buff going out, while also highlighting that 'six teams' finished under 1:21, a significant increase from 'three last year,' and mentioning that 'Indiana broke the American record.' The on-screen timer updates in real-time as swimmers complete their legs, culminating in Florida's victory, met with crowd reactions and their celebration in white and blue 'Gators' jerseys, emphasizing the competitive energy of the championships.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a high-angle view of a competitive swimming event, capturing multiple swimmers in mid-race during the freestyle leg of a relay, executing powerful strokes and turns in their respective lanes. The pool is marked with blue and white lane dividers, and the water churns with splashes as swimmers advance. Along the poolside, shirtless male swimmers in swim caps and goggles are seen preparing or recovering, while event officials in light-colored shirts observe closely. The camera then pans to a wider view of the arena, revealing the full scale of the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, with banners displaying 'NCAA' and 'SWIMMING & DIVING CHAMPIONSHIPS' lining the walls. The stands are filled with spectators, and a large digital scoreboard shows the event as 'MEN 200Y MEDLEY RELAY' with Florida leading. Swimmers from various teams, including those in black swimwear and hoodies (Florida State), white tracksuits (Texas), and black jackets (Tennessee), are visible near the starting blocks or in the pool. The atmosphere is intense and focused, with coaches and team members gathered along the deck, some clapping or gesturing. The camera captures the dynamic movement of swimmers turning at the wall and continuing their freestyle strokes, while the on-screen timer updates in real-time. The final moments show swimmers finishing their legs, with the Florida team emerging victorious, as indicated by the scoreboard and the crowd's reaction. The scene concludes with a wide shot of the arena, emphasizing the scale of the event and the competitive energy of the NCAA Championships.

segments.integrated_caption: The NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships unfold with a wide-angle view of a large indoor pool, its lanes marked by blue and white dividers, as the stands fill with cheering spectators and event banners line the poolside. Shirtless male swimmers, wearing caps and goggles, prepare on starting blocks for the 200Y Medley Relay. As the race begins with a synchronized dive and immediate transition to the butterfly stroke, creating splashes, commentators discuss a previous American record broken by a team, with one exclaiming, "How about that!" and another adding, "Should have mentioned that earlier, shouldn't we have?" The camera follows the swimmers, some gaining a slight lead, while the on-screen timer and leaderboard dynamically display the event name and current leader. As swimmers approach the first turn, executing sharp turns, the commentators focus on a specific swimmer's start, with one saying, "Watch right there... his feet... I think his foot is still on the block. I don't think that's a DQ." The other commentator then explains the electronic sensor system on the blocks that measures the precise time a toe leaves the block and hands touch the pad, aiming for a "perfect start" between .1 and .2 seconds, noting Omega's cameras for this. The race continues with intense competition and rhythmic strokes, capturing powerful movements and the engaged reactions of the spectators, whose background cheering is audible. Swimmers complete their legs, some turning at the wall for relay handoffs, as the on-screen display updates standings. The race culminates with determination, the final moments captured as swimmers reach the finish line, and the on-screen display shows the final results, including an NCAA Record for Florida. The crowd reacts with excitement, and the swimmers celebrate their performance, showcasing the intensity and skill of the 200Y Medley Relay.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, showcasing a large indoor pool with multiple lanes marked by blue and white lane dividers. The stands are filled with spectators, and banners for the event are visible along the poolside. Swimmers are positioned on starting blocks, shirtless and wearing swim caps and goggles, preparing for the 200Y Medley Relay. The race begins with a synchronized dive into the pool, and the swimmers immediately transition into the butterfly stroke, creating splashes as they propel forward. The camera follows the swimmers as they race down the pool, with some gaining a slight lead. The on-screen timer and leaderboard display the event name and current leader, updating dynamically. As the swimmers approach the first turn, they execute sharp turns at the wall, transitioning into the next stroke. The race continues with intense competition, and the swimmers maintain strong, rhythmic strokes. The camera captures the swimmers' powerful movements and the reactions of the spectators, who are visibly engaged in the race. The swimmers complete their legs, with some turning at the wall to continue the relay. The race progresses with the swimmers maintaining their positions, and the on-screen display updates with the current standings. The swimmers continue to race with determination, and the camera captures the final moments of the race as they approach the finish line. The race concludes with the swimmers completing their legs, and the on-screen display shows the final results, including the NCAA Record for Florida. The swimmers in the pool are seen finishing their legs, with some turning at the wall to complete their leg, and the race ends with the swimmers reaching the finish line. The crowd reacts with excitement, and the swimmers celebrate their performance. The video captures the intensity and excitement of the 200Y Medley Relay, with the swimmers demonstrating their skill and determination throughout the race.

Commentators explain that the precision of these timings is due to an electronic sensor system on the starting blocks that measures the exact moment a swimmer's toe leaves the block, aiming for a 'perfect start' between 0.1 and 0.2 seconds.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a swimmer executing a powerful freestyle stroke, creating a splash as they approach the wall, indicating the final leg of a relay race. The camera then pulls back to a wide-angle view of the 2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships at the University of Texas at Austin, revealing the pool with blue and white lane dividers and a bustling deck. Over this, two male commentators are heard discussing a previous event's electronic measurement, with one stating, "I didn't see it," and the other explaining, "there's a review because it doesn't have it on our monitor here." They debate the accuracy of the timing, noting, "most of the times it pops up .4, .2, it did not pop up." The arena features a 'CHAMPIONS WIN HERE' banner above sparsely populated spectator stands, with ESPN and NCAA logos visible. The commentators continue, mentioning "high-speed cameras" and the high stakes of a "national championship," speculating on a potential disqualification (DQ) for a swimmer, with one noting, "the longer they take, Ted, the more likely it is to be a DQ." They discuss how a DQ could mean "the fastest time in history is wiped off the map," though one expresses doubt. Arena staff and event officials move along the deck, managing equipment, as the camera pans across the pool, capturing digital signage. The scene transitions to a closer view of the starting blocks, where shirtless male swimmers in caps and goggles are being positioned. At this point, a distinct poolside announcer's voice is heard, confirming a disqualification: "the announcer just announced that Virginia DQ'd." The overall environment is professional and competitive, with a clear emphasis on the high-stakes nature of the NCAA Championships, as teams prepare for upcoming races.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a swimmer executing a powerful freestyle stroke in the pool, creating a splash as they approach the wall, indicating the final leg of a relay race. The camera then pulls back to a wide-angle view of the entire swimming arena, revealing the 2023 NCAA Division I Swimming & Diving Championships at the University of Texas at Austin. The pool is marked with blue and white lane dividers, and the deck is bustling with activity. Teams and officials are seen preparing for the next event, with swimmers and coaches gathered near the starting blocks. The 'CHAMPIONS WIN HERE' banner is prominently displayed above the spectator stands, which are sparsely populated. The ESPN and NCAA logos are visible in the top left corner, and the event branding is evident along the poolside. Arena staff and event officials move along the deck, managing equipment and coordinating logistics. The atmosphere is calm yet focused, with teams preparing for upcoming races. The camera pans slowly across the pool, capturing the organized chaos of a major collegiate swimming championship, with digital signage displaying event details and team names. The scene transitions to a slightly closer view of the starting blocks, where swimmers are being positioned, and the poolside announcer is seen speaking into a microphone, likely providing updates or commentary. The overall environment is professional and competitive, with a clear emphasis on the high-stakes nature of the NCAA Championships.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, showcasing a large indoor swimming arena with a multi-lane pool, starting blocks, and timing equipment. Banners like 'CHAMPIONS' and 'NCAA SWIMMOVE' adorn the venue, while spectators fill the stands, their presence marked by a continuous background hum of crowd noise. Event officials are seen at a long table, focused on laptops and notes, with one speaking into a microphone, coordinating the race. A formal male voice, identified as the Poolside Announcer, details race specifics, stating "Virginia Tech two lane four. Disqualification for early takeoff" and later mentioning "three lane five." The scene transitions to swimmers mid-race, executing powerful freestyle strokes, with a yellow circle highlighting a key competitor. A Color Commentator, a distinct male voice, expresses surprise with an emphatic "Oh! Florida's DQ'd!" as the disqualifications are announced. He elaborates on the significant impact, explaining, "Three disqualifications and the third one erases Florida's win and record setting time," and later exclaims, "Oh my gosh," lamenting the outcome and the "40 points that go to Texas." The commentator continues to express disbelief and difficulty in discerning details, addressing a colleague, "Look, look, Ted, it's it's really hard to see because you get the sense that it's toe, but it's hard to see because this," as coaches and support staff remain active on the pool deck, all under the watchful eye of ESPN and NCAA broadcast logos.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of an indoor swimming arena during the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, showcasing a large pool with multiple lanes marked by blue and white lane dividers. The poolside is lined with starting blocks, timing equipment, and officials. Spectators are visible in the stands, and banners for the event, including 'CHAMPIONS' and 'NCAA SWIMMOVE', are displayed. The camera then cuts to a close-up of the event officials seated at a long table, focused on their tasks—some are using laptops, others are writing notes, and one is speaking into a microphone, likely coordinating or announcing. The officials wear lanyards and casual attire, with one in a red shirt and another in a gray polo. The scene transitions back to the pool, where swimmers are mid-race, executing freestyle strokes with powerful kicks and arm movements. The camera captures the intensity of the race from an elevated angle, showing swimmers in various lanes, some leading, others trailing. A yellow circle highlights a swimmer in the top lane, possibly indicating a key competitor or a moment of interest. The pool deck is active with coaches and support staff observing, and the atmosphere is charged with competition. The ESPN and NCAA logos are visible in the corner, confirming the event’s broadcast and official status.

Florida's DQ'd!"), explaining the impact of disqualifications ("Three disqualifications... erases Florida's win and record setting time"), lamenting ("Oh my gosh"), and addressing a colleague named "Ted" while trying to interpret the visual details of the race.

segments.integrated_caption: The 200Y Medley Relay race is in full swing at the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, with shirtless male swimmers powering through the water in freestyle, creating visible splashes. A male announcer comments on an official being in the way of a swimmer's foot, while the ESPN Camera Crew captures the action. The announcer then expresses profound heartbreak for Florida, emphasizing the significance of this first race for building momentum, especially after breaking a record. Initial crowd cheers erupt, likely coinciding with the Florida Team's visible celebration in their white and blue 'Gators' jerseys. However, a second announcer dramatically reveals that Florida has been disqualified for an illegal exchange from the butterfly to the freestyle. The scene then cuts to an underwater view, where the On-Screen Timer and Leaderboard confirms the final results: Texas wins with a new NCAA Record of 1:20.28, followed by Tennessee and California, as spectators in the stands react to the unexpected outcome.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of the 200Y Medley Relay race in progress at the NCAA Swimming & Diving Championships, showing multiple swimmers in mid-stroke executing freestyle in their respective lanes, creating splashes as they power through the water. The poolside is bustling with activity: the Poolside Announcer, dressed in a white shirt and dark pants, stands near the starting blocks, speaking into a microphone, while the ESPN Camera Crew operates equipment to capture the race. Swimmers on Starting Blocks are seen diving in and swimming with intense focus. The scene then cuts to the Florida Team, now in white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, celebrating their victory with joyous gestures—waving to the crowd, hugging teammates, and displaying visible excitement. The camera then transitions to an underwater view of the pool, where the On-Screen Timer and Leaderboard is displayed, showing the final results: Texas wins with a time of 1:20.28 (marked as XR for NCAA Record), followed by Tennessee (1:20.50), California (1:20.76), and others. The scoreboard is overlaid on a blurred underwater shot of the pool lanes, emphasizing the conclusion of the event. Spectators in the stands are visible in the background during the celebration, reacting to the outcome.

entity_library.Florida State Team: Wearing black swimwear and hoodies, with swim caps and goggles; they walk out confidently, some with hands raised or gesturing, and are announced by commentators as being in lane one for the 200 Medley Relay.

entity_library.Branded Tunnels: Large, illuminated entryways with team names, logos, and swimmer names displayed prominently, emitting smoke for dramatic effect, though not visible in the current wide shot.
entity_library.California Team: Wearing black hoodies and swim trunks; one swimmer is shirtless with a towel, and they walk confidently past a large 'CALIFORNIA' banner with names like Seeliger, Okadome, Rose, and Alexy, referred to as 'Cal' by the announcer.
entity_library.NC State Team: Clad in white tracksuits with red accents and swim caps; one swimmer wears a red beanie, and they display focused, composed body language as they exit their branded tunnel, with their swimmer McCarty being highlighted by the announcer for a 9.89 split and taking an early lead.

entity_library.Race Announcer: Standing near the poolside, wearing a white shirt and dark pants, speaking into a microphone and providing live commentary during the final moments of the race, discussing a potential official review of a swimmer's start, detailing the Florida team's 'exchange from fly to free' (Liendo in, Scott Buff out), and providing statistics about team performances and records, all against the backdrop of an audible crowd.
entity_library.Florida Team Celebration: Swimmers in white and blue 'Gators' hockey-style jerseys with orange trim, waving to the crowd, hugging, and displaying joy after winning the NCAA Record in the 200Y Medley Relay, a victory enthusiastically confirmed and predicted by the Race Announcer.
